title: API Handling
description: Making HTTP requests, handling responses, parsing JSON and XML data, setting headers, managing errors, and working with authentication.
codeBlock:
# ---------- GET request ----------
import requests

response = requests.get("https://api.github.com/users/python")
print(response.status_code)       # 200
print(response.ok)                # True (status < 400)
print(response.headers["Content-Type"])

# Parse JSON response
data = response.json()            # dict / list
print(data["login"])
print(data["public_repos"])

# Query parameters
params = {"q": "python", "page": 1}
resp = requests.get("https://api.github.com/search/repositories", params=params)

# ---------- POST request ----------
payload = {"title": "Bug fix", "body": "Fixed the issue"}
resp = requests.post("https://api.example.com/issues", json=payload)
print(resp.status_code)           # 201 Created

# ---------- Headers & auth ----------
headers = {"Authorization": "Bearer YOUR_TOKEN", "Accept": "application/json"}
resp = requests.get("https://api.example.com/secure", headers=headers)

# Timeout (prevents hanging)
resp = requests.get("https://api.example.com", timeout=10)

# ---------- Error handling ----------
try:
    resp = requests.get("https://api.example.com/data", timeout=5)
    resp.raise_for_status()       # raises HTTPError for 4xx/5xx
    data = resp.json()
except requests.exceptions.Timeout:
    print("Request timed out")
except requests.exceptions.HTTPError as e:
    print(f"HTTP error: {e}")
except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e:
    print(f"Request failed: {e}")

# ---------- XML parsing (ElementTree) ----------
import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET

xml_data = """<book><title>Python 101</title><price>29.99</price></book>"""
root = ET.fromstring(xml_data)
print(root.tag)                   # "book"
title = root.find("title").text   # "Python 101"
price = root.find("price").text   # "29.99"

# Parse from file
tree = ET.parse("data.xml")
root = tree.getroot()
for item in root.findall(".//item"):
    print(item.text)

# ---------- JSON vs XML trade-offs ----------
# JSON: simple, fast, native Python types, preferred for REST APIs
# XML: namespaces, schemas (XSD), attributes, wider enterprise adoption
details:
The requests library is the de facto standard for HTTP in Python. It provides a clean, human-readable API for GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH, and HEAD requests. Responses expose .status_code, .headers, .json(), .text, and .content (raw bytes). The library automatically handles connection pooling, cookie persistence, redirect following, and gzip/deflate decompression. It is not part of the standard library — install with pip install requests.

For URL-only use (standard library), urllib.request provides lower-level HTTP access but requires more boilerplate for headers, encoding, and error handling. For async HTTP, see httpx (drop-in requests-compatible API with async/await support) or aiohttp for fully asynchronous workflows.

JSON parsing via response.json() deserializes the response body into native Python types (dict, list, str, int, float, bool, None) according to RFC 7159. The standard library json module provides identical functionality via json.loads() for string input and json.load() for file input. For high-throughput JSON processing, orjson and simdjson offer significant performance improvements.

XML parsing using xml.etree.ElementTree (standard library) provides a lightweight, Pythonic API. For advanced XML needs — schema validation (XSD), XPath/XSLT transformations, or DTD parsing — consider lxml, which is faster and more feature-complete. XML differs from JSON in supporting attributes (<item id="1">), namespaces (xmlns), mixed content, and document type declarations.

For data interchange format selection: JSON is preferred for REST APIs, configuration files, and most web services due to its simplicity and native JavaScript support. XML remains common in enterprise SOAP APIs, legacy systems, and document-centric workflows (SVG, RSS/Atom, Office Open XML). For structured tabular data, CSV or Parquet may be more appropriate.

See the requests library documentation, the json module, xml.etree.ElementTree, and lxml for complete documentation.
